999.note: UK emergency number for police, fire, or ambulance

flat white.note: A popular UK coffee order — espresso with steamed milk, similar to a latte but stronger and smaller.

take away.note: British English for 'to go' (American English). E.g. 'a takeaway coffee', 'shall we get a takeaway tonight?'

oat milk.note: A common dairy-free milk alternative, widely available in UK cafés.

grand.note: Informal British way of saying 'great' or 'that's fine'. Common in everyday chat, not about money here.

excuse me.note: Polite way to get someone's attention before asking a question.

station.note: Can mean train station or tube (underground) station depending on context.

round the corner.note: Means very close by. 'Round' is the British spelling/usage of 'around'.

you can't miss it.note: A common phrase when giving directions, meaning it's obvious/easy to spot.

hiya.note: A casual, friendly version of 'hi', very common among students and young people in the UK.

flatmate.note: British English for someone you share a flat/apartment with (American English: roommate).

a bit of a state.note: Informal way of saying something is messy or in poor condition.

what can i get for you.note: Friendly way to ask for an order

give us.note: Informal "give me/us" — common in service settings

give us a shout.note: Informal 'give me/us' — very British, means 'let me know'

then.note: Conversational filler at the end of a question, softens it

cuppa.note: Casual short for 'a cup of tea'

stick.note: Casual for 'put' or 'attach', e.g. 'stick a label on it'

washing up.note: British term for washing dishes (not laundry)

rota.note: A rota is a schedule/rotation list, e.g. for chores

your bit.note: Your share of something, e.g. a shared bill

spare.note: An extra one you're not currently using

help yourself.note: Invites someone to take/use something freely

mate.note: Very common British word for friend

the more the merrier.note: A set phrase welcoming more people to join

nabbed.note: Casual past tense of 'nab', meaning grabbed quickly

proper.note: British informal intensifier, e.g. 'proper busy' = very busy

on the blink.note: Informal way to say a device/appliance isn't working properly

pop a ticket in.note: Casual for submitting a maintenance request

sorry to bother you.note: Polite way to open a request to a stranger

carry on.note: Keep going in the same direction

postbox.note: British term for a street mailbox, usually bright red

get into town.note: 'Town' often means the town/city centre in British English

hop on.note: Casual way to say 'get on' a bus or train

not from round here.note: Explains unfamiliarity with the local area

walk you through.note: To explain something step by step

roundabout.note: Circular road junction, very common in the UK

tucked behind.note: Describes something set back from the main view, slightly hidden

cut through.note: To pass through a place as a shortcut

a bit lost.note: Softened way of admitting you don't know where you are

ever so much.note: British intensifier often paired with 'thanks', e.g. 'thanks ever so much'

sore throat.note: Common minor ailment asked about at a pharmacy

cold.note: Common illness, distinct from 'flu' which is more severe

as needed.note: Take medicine only when the symptom occurs, not on a fixed schedule

empty stomach.note: Without having eaten; relevant for medicine instructions

book an appointment.note: To schedule a time to see a doctor

a bit off.note: Mild, informal way to describe feeling unwell

it hurts.note: Used to describe pain when pointing to a spot

prescription.note: A doctor's written authorisation for medicine

pick up.note: To collect something, e.g. a prescription from the pharmacy

clear up.note: For symptoms or an illness to resolve

life-threatening.note: Describes a serious, urgent medical condition

on its way.note: Something (e.g. help) is currently travelling towards you

all of a sudden.note: Describes something happening unexpectedly and quickly

stay calm.note: Common reassurance during a stressful or urgent situation

call in sick.note: To notify someone you won't attend due to illness

get well soon.note: Common well-wish said to someone who is ill

a&e.note: Accident & Emergency — the UK term for the ER

gp surgery.note: British term for a local doctor's practice/clinic

is this seat taken.note: Polite way to check before sitting down in a lecture or seminar

didn't quite catch it.note: Polite way to ask someone to repeat what they said

seminar.note: Small-group discussion class, distinct from a lecture

stuck on.note: Unable to progress past a particular point

office hours.note: Set times when a tutor/lecturer is available for questions

on the right track.note: Making good progress in the right direction

deadline.note: The date work must be submitted by

extension.note: Extra time granted to submit work late

student portal.note: The university's online system for grades, enrolment, etc.

wifi.note: Wireless internet, commonly needed across campus

print.note: To produce a paper copy, often needed for essays/handouts

reserve.note: To book something in advance, e.g. a study room

swap numbers.note: Exchanging contact details, common when arranging group work

society.note: A student club based around a shared interest

workload.note: The amount of academic work you have to do

back to back.note: One after another with no break in between

sign up.note: To register for something, e.g. a module or event

good luck.note: Common well-wish before an exam or challenge

freshers' fair.note: An event at the start of term where new students explore clubs/societies

bit chilly.note: Mild, understated way to say it's cold

typical british weather.note: Common jokey remark about how quickly UK weather changes

make the most of.note: To take full advantage of something while you can

alright.note: Very common casual British greeting, often said as a question

long time no see.note: Said when meeting someone you haven't seen in a while

can't complain.zh: 还不错/没什么好抱怨的
can't complain.note: Casual, modest way of saying things are fine

treating you.note: Used in 'how's X treating you', asking how an experience has been

fancy.note: Casual way to suggest or ask if someone wants something

up to.note: Used in 'what are you up to', asking about someone's plans/activities

pencil in.note: To tentatively schedule something, open to change later

well up for.note: Casual enthusiastic way of agreeing to something

sounds good.note: Casual way to agree to a plan

can't make it.note: Polite way to decline an invitation due to unavailability

rain check.note: Idiom for postponing an invitation to a later date

what are you having.note: Common phrase when offering to buy someone a drink

my round.note: Pub custom: taking a turn to buy drinks for the group

cheers.note: Used both as a toast and as a casual thank-you

same again.note: Ordering another of the same drink

having a laugh.note: Used in 'you're having a laugh', meaning 'you must be joking'

give over.note: Informal British way of telling someone to stop, often in jest

well into.note: Enthusiastic about a hobby or interest at the moment

brilliant.note: Very common British word for excellent/great

rubbish.note: Informal British word for something bad or of poor quality

fair enough.note: Casual acceptance of a reason or explanation, even if not fully agreeing

be a pain.note: Used in 'I don't want to be a pain', softening a request

no bother at all.note: Reassurance that a request or favour is not a problem

a bit much.note: Understated way of saying something is excessive

taking forever.note: Informal complaint about something being slow

in the queue.note: 'Queue' is the British word for a line of waiting people

heard a lot about you.note: Polite phrase said when meeting someone you've heard about before

best be off.note: Casual British way of saying it's time to leave

take care.note: Warm, common way to say goodbye

just browsing.note: Common reply when a shop assistant offers help, meaning you don't need assistance yet

try on.note: To put on clothing to check fit before buying

fitting rooms.note: British term for changing rooms in a clothes shop

size.note: UK clothing sizes differ from US/EU, worth double-checking

another colour.note: Asking if an item comes in a different colour option

too tight.note: Describes clothing that doesn't fit comfortably

in the sale.note: British phrase for something reduced in price ('the sale' = discount period)

% off.note: Used to state a percentage discount, e.g. '20% off'

apple pay.note: Mobile contactless payment method, widely accepted in the UK

pay by card.note: Paying with a debit or credit card rather than cash

declined.note: When a card payment fails to go through

pin.note: Personal Identification Number used to authorise card payments

return.note: Bringing an item back to the shop, usually for a refund or exchange

receipt.note: Proof of purchase, often required for returns

refund.note: Getting your money back for a returned item

tag.note: The label attached to new clothing, often needed intact for returns

bag.note: In UK supermarkets you're often asked if you need one, sometimes at a small charge

loyalty card.note: A card used to collect points/discounts at a specific shop

self-checkout.note: Machines that let customers scan and pay without a cashier

unexpected item.note: Famous self-checkout error message ('unexpected item in the bagging area')

open a bank account.note: Common early task for international students settling in the UK

bank statement.note: A document showing account transactions over a period

balance.note: The amount of money currently in an account

skint.note: Very common British informal word for having little money

bargain.note: Something bought for a good, low price
